sentences.text: Yes.
Was in Moravia, in [NON-ENGLISH].
And he has a house there.
And Mrs.
Schindler, she live in this house.
Sometime she was in the window and watching us when we've been outside.
I-- I-- not-- just after war.
I-- I mean, in the museum, I met her.
But I saw her from the window.
This time she was a young lady.
Now she's old.
Very kind, very nice lady.
She did have some wool.
I don't know where she took the wool and to give to us, so we been knitting sweaters for us because we've been not busy.
So this was a good-- good for us to knit.
And was winter, so was good to have the sweaters.
That we been busy, or we wasn't busy?
[INAUDIBLE]
We no--
You mean not busy?
Yeah, not busy because we didn't work a lot in this factory.
So we woke up early, like 5 o'clock, went-- went to the appell.
And then walk to the factory.
And then it depends.
When I work for the Emalia, so we-- we-- we putted this-- I told you this--
In the first factory.
But now you ask about the Czechoslovakia factory.
Yeah, so they been big machines.
And I-- nobody trained me.
I didn't know how to operate the machines.
Was not the enamel work anymore, just the arms work.
And--
I don't know.
They say that the V-- this was the-- the newest-- that we work on the-- the V arms.
It was the newest.
What-- was very powerful.
The German find out.
But then we-- we been hearing that it not work, nothing what we did.
They just say arms and V.
The very modern somethings.
Somebody find out.
So this is what he got to do in this factory.
Just sit by a tall machine and was like a-- a tube.
And you put around metal there and-- and push with your legs, and then down.
Very boring work.
But was not too much.
Then we didn't do nothing.
We've been sitting around. we were just talking.
So we have the sign.
And we make six.
If somebody noticed him or another person, some German or woman German, so they make the sign six.
And everybody went to his place.
We didn't talk.
We pretend we work hard.
And that this what we did.
Just we've been cooking, [LAUGHS] just about food.
Mostly woman been talking and about hoping that one time the war will finish, and we will be with our families.
Yeah, always even, for example, where you sleep.
So in the room is lots of girls.
And we make friends.
We've been friends.
Just be optimistic.
We been and talking about end the war, talking about good food, what we will cook when we come back.
So somebody say, potatoes and sour milk.
This-- [LAUGHS] somebody like meat, we talk how we cook and what we will eat when we come back home-- stuff like this.
Just the girl who was the poet, she-- she write the poems.
And she-- she talk about this.
And she was very good.
Yes, yes.
About surviving, about nature, about people.
Were very good poems.
No.
No, was not other activities-- nothing.
It depends.
You have very religious people when it was Yom Kippur, so they've been fasting.
They've been fasting, yeah.
Shabbat, you couldn't keep Shabbat because you have to go to the factories.
So Saturday, you have to work.
When it was nice sun, so we went outside to the-- on sun.
But this was only at Schindler, not-- not in Plaszow.
We were not going, sit in the-- take sunshine.
No.
No.
But-- yeah, but very quick, we make friends.
So this was not problem.
Out
The people?
Mostly, they've been in school.
And in concentration camp, they interrupt school.
So--
No.
But was there one doctor.
One surgeon was there.
And one beautiful girl-- she was so sick.
She got diarhhea, and she died.
They give her, but-- but she didn't survive.
Beautiful girl.
She was so beautiful that the German said he will make for her special place a bury-- to bury her.
Well, yeah, the one German who was in charge.
He didn't put her with the grave where, you know, the mass grave.
Just, he make a special place for her.
No.
I don't.
No, I don't know.
Just I know she got diarhhea.
And it was not-- maybe they will cure her now, you know.
But-- but I was also sick.
I was-- I have also diarrhea.
But we have a doctor.
His name was Dr.
Rubenstein.
And [?
Sulina ?] was his nurse.
He too had been for us.
Yeah, they've been prisoners, too.
And I was really very sick and went to him.
And they didn't give me any medicine.
Just he put me on diet eat apples.
And they got apples for me.
And this cure me.
Few days.
And then I was OK again.
They allow me to rest, yeah.
This was under Schindler, so-- so--
I-- I couldn't go to work, I was so weak.
I-- I have to be in bed.
Yeah.
Yeah, they get sick.
We-- this why we have the doctor.
Yeah, one doctor, and he has a nurse.
Sulina ?] was her name.
Oh, no.
No.
No, but two brothers been there, very religious guys.
And them been talking about holy days and were praying.
They probably pray, but I doubt that they have some prayers books or something.
Tallis, I doubt that.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They probably didn't have any tallis because they went through Bergen-Belsen.
This was a big camp.
And they have to take off the-- the clothes.
Well, we've been in his camp in Moravia.
Still, he was always great guy.
He was always very nice to us, and good looking, and very polite, kind.
We just love him.
Everybody loves him a lot.
Always good word to everybody.
And he-- and he wasn't scared, you know this?
What I wondered that somebody can tell on him and take away the factory from him.
But nobody was against him He just have friends.
And he tried to have the-- the Nazis on his side.
So he bribed them and party with them.
And-- and he could arrange everything what he wanted.
For better or for worse?
No, he was always all the same.
And when the Russian came, I saw him in there from the yard and surrounded with the Jewish elderly.
And he was very--
The Jewish elderly surround him.
Elderly people.
Seniors.
And he was trembling.
He was shaking.
He was so scared.
But they took him to Palestine.
And they-- he-- they say, you save us.
Now w we save your life.
And they ask the Russian commandant, they-- they explain that he wasn't a regular Nazi.
He has the swastika still.
Yes.
And--
And the commandant, the-- was a Jewish guy.
And in the-- in the Jewish language he say, [YIDDISH].
You not-- will go back to-- to Poland.
He's big anti-Semitist.
Just go to-- to Israel.
This was Palestine this time.
Yeah, this was the end.
Pardon?
Just when the liberation started.
So the-- but what was interesting, that the German been digging a hole for us, a big hole, the Nazi.
No Schindler.
Just the Nazi.
They knew is the Jewish camp there.
So they've been digging a big hole to put us there and to shoot us.
And the-- and this was in Czechoslovakia.
So the Czech partisan came.
And they find out this.
And they start-- start to hear, the Russian is coming.
And this was not true.
They came later, around maybe two, three days later.
That they knew they are going to-- to liberate us.
The partisans-- the Czech partisans.
And they've been hearing, Russian are coming.
Russian are coming.
And you know the German, they-- they've been good in killing the-- the [INAUDIBLE] people who didn't have arms.
But they-- how you call these people who scared?
How you call-- what?
Cowards.
They been big coward, so they start to run away.
And so they save our lives.
Otherwise, they-- they will shoot us, so the Czech partisans, they save our lives.
They knew about the hole what they are digging for us.
We needed lots of miracles, you know, and this was one of the miracles, too.
That-- that they didn't take me to-- to the gas chamber was a miracle.
That I went-- that I been with Schindler, and he protect us-- this was a miracle.
So--
He was in the back yard and-- and-- and scared and tremble.
So-- so they--
Because he was a Nazi.
He has a swastika in his-- on his garment.
And the-- the-- the Jewish, elderly explain to them.
Stop?
Then he was a good Nazi, that he save us all.
So they-- they didn't kill him.
Yeah, I was there.
This-- this what they saying.
He was a good-- he was good man.
He not deserve to be killed because he save all these people here.
And they understand, and they didn't touch him no more.
To the Russian.
Yeah.
He was-- yeah, he was a Jewish commander.
And he talk in Yiddish to us.
[YIDDISH] You not go-- I don't know Yiddish to say.
But you-- you don't go back to Poland.
It's not good for you.
Just go to Palestine.
Next was freedom.
Then we went home.
Then--
--we look home.
This was end-- this was end of the war, what-- what I would describe now.
And everybody went however they could-- a little by train, a little walk, a little cars.
And soldiers took us.
And took maybe one-- one week to get home.
Just in-- I didn't have nothing.
These shoes-- what you see here, what I have the-- the high shoes are-- was ripped.
Always, I have the same shoes.
And always we've been scared that you will not lose these shoes.
What to do if you don't have shoes, and only the one shoe?
They've been ripped completely.
If the war is longer, I would be barefoot.
There was not a store that you can buy shoes.
You didn't have money even if were-- if there would be a store.
When I went back?
Well, it's a group of boys and girls and.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And on the-- how we walk back home, or-- or travel, the Czech people give us food.
They've been standing with food, bread, and tried to help.
They've been nice.
They knew we'd been prisoners.
First, I go to Zakopane and To see where there is somebody back.
Was nobody back.
I went back to Cracow.
And there, I've been going every day and searching, searching, asking people and--
For my family, whether they came back.
Looking for them, and unfortunately, no-- nobody.
But they told me that my cousin came back from Canada.
Herman is his name.
And they told me in what city.
And I was there also looking for family.
So we met together.
And he say he look for family.
I say, I look for family.
In Palestine, in Israel.
In Canada, he is now.
He-- this his home in Canada, in Montreal.
But during the war, he was in--
He-- during the war, he was in English [INAUDIBLE]-- and on motorcycle they went-- on motorcycle.
Yeah.
But English-- from English soldier.
Yeah.
No, then he wents-- he went to Canada.
And I stay in Cracow.
But he-- first, he arrange for me a sanatorium after war, that I recover.
And there I met my husband.
It-- it was in the High Tatra in the mountains for tuberculose.
But they clean up this and for us for the-- for the survivors.
They make a place.
This was UNRRA, a American organization.
And they brought food and clothes-- lots of clothes we can choose, shoes.
And food for three weeks, it was arranged that you-- you are, for free, can eat and have a room.
Three weeks.
And there I met my husband.
Somebody told me that here is a young lawyer, and he would like to meet me.
What did I want?
I say, OK.
And so he started to talk to me.
And I-- I was from Poland, and I-- he's from Czechoslovakia.
And I didn't know this language, Slovak, or Czech.
But he knew German, and I knew German.
So we communicate in German.
And he says, I have nobody, only old mother and a brother.
If you have nowhere to go, you welcome come to my house.
So I went to Poland, liquidate what I have there, take with me the little stuff, and came to him.
No, just a little stuff, what I have with me.
And--
Nothing.
Nothing I found in this home.
Just stuff what after-- after the liberation they gave us clothes and small stuff.
This I have with me.
So--
He was in Hungarian, in Hungaria on forget papers.
Forged papers, yeah.
How he was living there?
In fear.
Very hard.
Very hard.
And he has his mother there and brother and take care of them.
He was the youngest.
And they walked through the mountains to get there.
He hire-- they hired somebody who knew the way.
And they pay for it.
And-- and they'd been hiding.
How they earn money?
They did have some money from before.
Yeah.
For-- for a while, I stayed in the house.
And we knew each other better.
And then he proposed to me, and we married.
In Martin, it called, in Slovakia.
Then I didn't go back to Poland no more.
Very good.
Very good.
I-- I didn't have any problem.
I learn first the language because it's-- it's similar to Polish, you know?
Yeah, there'd been.
It was a community.
And he open his store with his father, was with liquor.
Yeah.
So he opened this.
And then we have a good living there.
Yeah, we did have.
And we want to go.
But Sonia, my daughter, she-- I was pregnant and have a baby.
And then come news that it's very hard life in there with a little baby.
That is too risky to go.
So then we didn't go.
Then when the kids been bigger, then we'd immigrate to Israel.
You mean when I was married?
My husband, he wasn't very religious.
And I-- I have problems after war to believe in-- in some-- in God or believe something, you know?
It was very hard for me.
But then I realized that be like a wild animal and not believe in nothing is worse than believe.
So I-- I started to be a believer.
I started to go to synagogue to pray, and still I am in Temple Emanuel.
Belong to Temple Emanuel.
Well, I felt that I-- I-- I was in dilemma.
I didn't know what to do.
First I say, cannot be God if he could do this to me.
But then [INAUDIBLE] God was also not good.
So-- so I stay with my religion.
I was born Jewish, and I-- I still Jewish.
I practice.
You saw me took the candle.
Pardon?
I will be arrive in Israel?
Well, very hard.
Very hard because come from communist country, you need some money to start.
And didn't let us take any money.
So it was very, very hard.
We find work.
We have-- there's, in Be'er Sheva.
They put us in Be'er Sheva.
And I find out that somebody from [NON-ENGLISH], from Poland, is there.
[NON-ENGLISH] was his name.
And he has a-- a hotel and coffeehouse.
And he give me work to do.
Was hard for me, but-- but I have a job.
And then my husband find a job.
And the kids went to kibbutz, all three.
So-- so we start.
But was not easy.
No.
We've been in Be'er Sheva, and the kids went to kibbutz.
Four years.
And here in this house live my brother-- live my husband brother.
And he wrote us letters that he's very sick and whether I can come and help him.
I didn't know what to do.
But family decided that yeah, that I have to go because they-- he was my husband.
Stop?
Continue.
He want my husband here to come, his brother.
But Paul say, what-- what's father can help?
He wasn't-- he didn't know how to cook and to clean and take care of a sick person.
They decided that I will be the-- the person who can-- he can use me.
So I came here by myself.
All my family, I leave there.
And I have [INAUDIBLE]-- you know [INAUDIBLE], what it is, a Zionistic Jewish organization-- I have a job there.
So I leave the job, my family behind, and came here.
Pardon?
They came maybe-- I came-- I think in one year, they came.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And all three, they came-- Julius, my son, Paul, and my husband.
Sonia, she was married, so she-- she stayed there.
